ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general risotto food product class; no specific mushroom risotto entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers risotto as a category; no specific porcini mushroom risotto sub-entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for mushroom risotto. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mushroom risotto is approximately 350 g, served as a main course on a plate.

nutrient_source.title: Risotto, mushroom — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Risotto ai Funghi with Carnaroli rice and porcini mushrooms exists in USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked Arborio/Carnaroli-style risotto (FDC ID 169718 cooked white rice, short-grain), mushroom contributions (FDC ID 169242 porcini mushrooms, dried, reconstituted), and Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171247), weighted to a typical restaurant-style recipe proportion (approximately 65% cooked rice, 15% mushrooms, 10% Parmesan, 10% butter/oil/stock). Values are per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Risotto ai Funghi with Carnaroli rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: white short-grain rice risotto (GI ~54, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021). Carnaroli rice has a similar starch structure to Arborio rice used in GI-tested risotto preparations. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 71.75) / 100 ≈ 38.7, rounded to 38.5, based on 350 g serving with 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 980 mg sodium, representing about 43% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load and refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 54 and a glycemic load of ~38.5 per standard serving, mushroom risotto produces a moderate-to-high postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from Parmesan cheese and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (~7.7 g per serving from butter and Parmesan) and elevated sodium (~980 mg per serving) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing reduced-butter preparations and smaller portions can help manage intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Risotto ai Funghi is a classic northern Italian rice dish made by slowly cooking Carnaroli or Arborio short-grain rice in warm vegetable or chicken stock, stirring continuously to release the starch and create a creamy, velvety texture without the use of cream. The dish is flavoured with porcini mushrooms — either dried and rehydrated or fresh — which impart a deep, earthy, umami-rich flavour. Parmesan cheese (Parmigiano-Reggiano) and butter are stirred in at the end in a technique called mantecatura, giving the risotto its characteristic glossy richness. The vegetarian version (V) omits meat-based stock and uses vegetable broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 4.5 g fat (of which 2.2 g is saturated), and 20.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 350 g restaurant serving is substantial at approximately 38.5, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate-to-high due to Parmesan and stock, relevant for hypertension management. The dish contains dairy (Parmesan, butter) and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance. It is a culturally significant dish in Italian cuisine, particularly associated with the Lombardy and Piedmont regions, and is widely served in Italian restaurants globally including in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in risotto ai funghi?
What is the GI of risotto?
How much sodium is in mushroom risotto?
What rice is used in risotto?
Is Carnaroli rice the same as Arborio?
Can I eat risotto if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Risotto ai Funghi with Carnaroli rice and porcini mushrooms was found in any primary approved source. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked short-grain white rice, porcini mushrooms, and Parmesan cheese in typical recipe proportions. GI was estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (white short-grain rice risotto, GI ~54). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved-source entry for Risotto ai Funghi exists. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a more specific approved composite entry if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients at typical preparation levels. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on white short-grain rice risotto from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 5. The (V) suffix in the input food name indicates a vegetarian preparation; is_vegetarian is set to true and vegetable stock is assumed. 6. Serving size of 350 g is based on a typical Italian restaurant main-course portion. 7. Sodium estimate of 280 mg per 100 g reflects stock and Parmesan contributions; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_00002694 is used as closest parent for risotto food product; no specific mushroom risotto sub-class exists in FoodOn as of the accessed date. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for risotto; verified against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser. 10. This dish is Italian cuisine; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes do not apply. Local names fields are null as Italian is a Latin-script language and the canonical food name is already in Italian.
